_comment: Semantic component investigation contract (captain-atlas 17). Fixture-backed for this slice: the data shape is producer-compatible (a future producer overwrites this file with real output). Every claim carries a source_boundary and a resolvable source_ref. The Spark<->Flink overlap is declared on BOTH components. Component ids match docs/site/atlas/system-map.demo.json. Swap this sample for another eligible set by editing only this fixture + sources.json — the contract, validator, shell, and harness are data-driven.

regions.label: Search region
regions.description: Components that index and query documents over large corpora.

regions.label: Compute & streaming region
regions.description: Components that move and transform data across a cluster. Overlap lives here: Spark and Flink both process big data but with different streaming models.

regions.label: Orchestration region
regions.description: Components that schedule and coordinate work, including retired/legacy schedulers with an honest source boundary.

regions.label: Platform foundation region
regions.description: The Hadoop filesystem/YARN foundation the other regions build on.

sample.selection_reason: Four components prove the portable contract without encoding product facts around fixed names: Solr (a component with a clear search/indexing capability and a rich internal model), Spark and Flink (a meaningful semantic overlap pair in the compute/streaming region, bidirectionally related across 3+ dimensions), and Oozie (a retired, metadata-only component that forces an honest not_assessed/curated source boundary). The selection exercises every required relation type and source boundary; swapping it for another eligible set requires editing only this fixture and the source cards.

purpose.summary: Solr is the ecosystem's search and indexing server: it ingests documents into an inverted index and answers term, boolean, and ranked queries at scale. In Bigtop it is the search capability, packaged as a versioned component and wired into the smoke-test and deployment surface.

internal_concepts.label: Inverted index
internal_concepts.explanation: Documents are tokenized into an inverted index (term to postings). This is what makes term and boolean queries fast; schema/analyser choices directly affect recall and ranking.

internal_concepts.label: Managed schema
internal_concepts.explanation: Field definitions, analysers, and tokenisers declared in a schema. The schema decides how text is split and matched; a wrong analyser is a query-quality bug, not a code bug.

internal_concepts.label: Query / parser
internal_concepts.explanation: Queries are parsed (standard or eDisMax) against the schema. Relevance functions (BM25) and boosts live here, separate from the index.

internal_concepts.label: Shard
internal_concepts.explanation: A collection is split into shards, each a horizontal slice of the index, sharded by document hash. The shard count is fixed at collection creation and expensive to change.

internal_concepts.label: Replica & SolrCloud coordination
internal_concepts.explanation: Each shard has replicas (leaders/followers). SolrCloud uses ZooKeeper and an Overseer node to elect leaders and route updates, so coordination is an operational concern, not just an index concern.

integration_surfaces.label: Bigtop Solr packaging recipe
integration_surfaces.explanation: Bigtop packages Solr as a versioned component (bigtop-packages/ Solr recipe); the version is pinned in the BOM.

integration_surfaces.label: Solr HTTP query API
integration_surfaces.explanation: Solr exposes an HTTP request API for queries and updates; clients integrate over HTTP.

integration_surfaces.label: SolrCloud deployment topology
integration_surfaces.explanation: A SolrCloud deployment needs ZooKeeper ensembles and shard/replica planning; the exact deployed topology in this target is not verified from the static corpus.

semantic_relations.explanation: SolrCloud depends on ZooKeeper for cluster coordination and leader election.
Solr can store its index on HDFS and integrates with the Hadoop filesystem layer.
Bigtop packages Solr into the distribution as a versioned, smoke-tested component.

risks.label: Shard count is expensive to change
risks.explanation: The number of shards is fixed at collection creation. Underestimating it forces a costly reindex/re-split as data grows, which is a capacity-planning trap specific to Solr.

risks.label: Deployed topology not statically verifiable
risks.explanation: The exact SolrCloud topology (shard/replica placement, ZooKeeper ensemble) cannot be confirmed from packaging manifests alone; a runtime/deploy probe is needed. Treating the recipe as the topology would be a false-completeness bug.

risks.label: HDFS-backed index performance assumption
risks.explanation: Storing the index on HDFS trades local-disk latency for cluster storage; assuming HDFS-backed indexing performs like local storage is a component-specific operational risk.

next_expedition.action: Capture the actual SolrCloud topology (shards/replicas/ZooKeeper ensemble) from a running instance in an approved sandbox.
next_expedition.why: Turns the agent-hypothesis deploy-topology risk into local-corpus/runtime evidence.

next_expedition.action: Pin the exact Bigtop Solr recipe version and any applied patches by scanning bigtop-packages/src/solr/.
next_expedition.why: Confirms the packaging integration surface at source level.

purpose.summary: Spark is a distributed data-processing engine for batch and micro-batch workloads over large datasets. In Bigtop it is the primary compute engine in the compute/streaming region, used for ETL, analytics, and structured streaming.

internal_concepts.label: RDD
internal_concepts.explanation: The Resilient Distributed Dataset: a fault-tolerant, partitioned, lazily-evaluated collection. The original low-level API and the lineage basis for recovery.

internal_concepts.label: DataFrame / Dataset
internal_concepts.explanation: The higher-level tabular API with a schema, over RDDs. Most production code uses DataFrames because the optimizer can reason about them.

internal_concepts.label: Catalyst optimizer
internal_concepts.explanation: Turns DataFrame operations into an optimized physical plan. It is why logically-equivalent pipelines can have very different performance.

internal_concepts.label: DAG scheduler
internal_concepts.explanation: Breaks a job into stages of tasks along shuffle boundaries and submits them. Stage count and shuffle volume dominate job performance.

internal_concepts.label: Executor
internal_concepts.explanation: A JVM process on a worker that runs tasks and holds cached data. Memory pressure (cache vs shuffle vs user objects) is the most common operational failure.

integration_surfaces.label: Bigtop Spark packaging recipe
integration_surfaces.explanation: Bigtop packages Spark as a versioned component; the version is pinned in the BOM and the support matrix.

integration_surfaces.label: Spark session / DataFrame API
integration_surfaces.explanation: Spark is driven through a SparkSession building DataFrames; this is the integration surface application code targets.

integration_surfaces.label: YARN / cluster deployment
integration_surfaces.explanation: Spark runs on YARN (Hadoop) as the cluster manager in this ecosystem; the depends_on Hadoop relation reflects this.

semantic_relations.explanation: Spark runs against Hadoop's filesystem and typically uses YARN as its cluster manager.

Spark and Flink are both distributed compute engines over partitioned data in the same compute/streaming region, both depend on Hadoop, and both are packaged by Bigtop. They overlap in problem space, ecosystem placement, and API surface.
Spark uses micro-batch streaming (throughput/simple), Flink is a true streaming engine (low latency, stateful exactly-once). This is the decisive axis that justifies the ecosystem containing both.

risks.label: Executor memory pressure
risks.explanation: Spark executors share memory across caching, shuffling, and user objects. Memory mis-sizing causes spilling and OOMs that look like data bugs — a component-specific failure mode.

risks.label: Shuffle skew dominates performance
risks.explanation: Because the DAG scheduler plans stages around shuffle boundaries, a skewed key distribution silently makes a job run far longer than its data size suggests. This is a Spark-specific tuning trap.

next_expedition.action: Capture Spark job stage/shuffle metrics from a representative run to validate the shuffle-skew hypothesis.
next_expedition.why: Turns the shuffle-skew agent hypothesis into measured evidence.

purpose.summary: Flink is a stream-processing engine (with a batch API layered on streaming) for stateful, low-latency pipelines. In Bigtop it sits beside Spark in the compute/streaming region as the true-streaming alternative.

internal_concepts.label: DataStream
internal_concepts.explanation: The core streaming API: a possibly-unbounded stream of events transformed through a pipeline. Flink models batch as a bounded stream, not the other way around.

internal_concepts.label: Job graph
internal_concepts.explanation: The pipeline compiles to a JobGraph of operators executed by a JobManager (coordination) and TaskManagers (workers). This is the runtime shape of a Flink job.

internal_concepts.label: Checkpoint
internal_concepts.explanation: Fault tolerance via distributed snapshots (checkpoint barriers flowing through the stream). This is the basis of Flink's exactly-once guarantee.

internal_concepts.label: State backend
internal_concepts.explanation: Operator state (keyed/operator) is stored in a state backend (e.g. RocksDB). Stateful streaming correctness depends on how state is keyed, checkpointed, and restored.

internal_concepts.label: Windowing
internal_concepts.explanation: Window operators group unbounded streams into finite windows (tumbling/sliding/session) over event/processing time. Window + watermark semantics are where most correctness bugs live.

integration_surfaces.label: Bigtop Flink packaging recipe
integration_surfaces.explanation: Bigtop packages Flink as a versioned component; the version is pinned in the BOM.

integration_surfaces.label: DataStream / Table API
integration_surfaces.explanation: Flink is driven through the DataStream and Table APIs; this is the integration surface for streaming applications.

integration_surfaces.label: JobManager/TaskManager deployment
integration_surfaces.explanation: A Flink deployment needs JobManager/TaskManager sizing and a state backend choice; the exact deployed topology is not verified from the static corpus.

semantic_relations.explanation: Flink can use Hadoop's filesystem for state checkpoints and YARN as a cluster manager.

Flink and Spark are both distributed compute engines over partitioned data in the same compute/streaming region, both depend on Hadoop, and both are packaged by Bigtop. They overlap in problem space, ecosystem placement, and API surface.
Flink is a true streaming engine (event-at-a-time, low latency, stateful exactly-once); Spark uses micro-batch streaming. From Flink's side the contrast is the same streaming/state/latency trade-off.

risks.label: Stateful correctness depends on state design
risks.explanation: Because Flink's exactly-once guarantee rests on how operator state is keyed and checkpointed, a subtle keying mistake produces silently-wrong results that still checkpoint cleanly. This is a Flink-specific correctness trap.

risks.label: Deployed topology not statically verifiable
risks.explanation: The JobManager/TaskManager layout and state backend cannot be confirmed from packaging manifests alone; a runtime probe is needed. Assuming the recipe reflects the deployment is a false-completeness bug.

next_expedition.action: Capture the actual JobManager/TaskManager topology and state backend from a running Flink cluster in an approved sandbox.
next_expedition.why: Turns the deploy-topology agent hypothesis into runtime evidence.

purpose.summary: Oozie was the Hadoop workflow scheduler: it ran DAGs of Hadoop actions (MapReduce, Hive, Pig, shell) on time/data triggers. In Bigtop it is the retired/legacy orchestration component with a metadata-only source boundary.

internal_concepts.label: Workflow (hPDL DAG)
internal_concepts.explanation: A DAG of Hadoop actions (MapReduce, Pig, Hive, fs, shell) defined in XML (hPDL). Control nodes (start/end/fork-join/decision) structure the flow.

internal_concepts.label: Coordinator
internal_concepts.explanation: A time/data trigger that schedules recurring workflow runs (frequency plus data-availability predicates). The scheduling layer on top of workflows.

internal_concepts.label: Bundle
internal_concepts.explanation: A higher-level grouping of coordinators, to manage a pipeline of pipelines together.

internal_concepts.label: HCatalog integration
internal_concepts.explanation: Oozie can trigger on Hive table partition availability via HCatalog, tying workflows to the Hive metastore.

internal_concepts.label: Runtime execution semantics
internal_concepts.explanation: Not_assessed: how the current workflow XML actually executes at runtime cannot be verified from this corpus because Oozie is retired and metadata-visible only. A runtime/deploy probe would be needed, but the project's retired status makes that low value.

integration_surfaces.label: Bigtop Oozie packaging recipe (metadata-only)
integration_surfaces.explanation: Oozie appears in the Bigtop BOM/support matrix as a packaged component, but only metadata-visible; the recipe source is not pinned.

integration_surfaces.label: Oozie REST/HTTP API
integration_surfaces.explanation: Oozie exposed an HTTP API to submit and monitor workflows; documented but not runtime-verified in this corpus.

integration_surfaces.label: Live deployment
integration_surfaces.explanation: Whether a live Oozie server runs in this target is not_assessed; the project is retired (Apache Attic).

semantic_relations.explanation: Oozie scheduled Hadoop actions (MapReduce, Hive) and depended on the Hadoop cluster to execute them.
Bigtop packaged Oozie into the distribution while it was active; it is retained as a retired/legacy component.

risks.label: Retired project (Apache Attic)
risks.explanation: Oozie is retired and moved to the Apache Attic: no new releases or fixes. Relying on it for new pipelines is a maintenance dead-end — a component-specific lifecycle risk the investigation must surface, not hide.

risks.label: Packaging recipe not source-pinned
risks.explanation: Because Oozie is metadata-visible only in this corpus, the exact recipe version and any applied patches are not pinned at source. Treating the BOM entry as the recipe would be a false-completeness bug.

next_expedition.action: Scan bigtop-packages/src/oozie/ to pin the exact recipe version and patches, turning the metadata-only package surface into a source-anchored one.
next_expedition.why: Closes the recipe-source-anchor gap and de-risks the metadata-only boundary.

next_expedition.action: Confirm the retired/Attic status and whether any live Oozie instance remains in the target.
next_expedition.why: Resolves whether the not_assessed runtime gap is a real unknown or simply 'confirmed retired, not running'.
